hypothesis: No prior run data exists; running the current architecture unmodified produces the reference val_accuracy, parameter_count, model_bytes, and latency_ms that all future mutations will be compared against.
mutation_summary: Keep model.py unchanged to establish the first baseline metrics for the tiny depthwise-residual CNN.
next_belief: The dominant bottleneck is representational capacity per parameter, not size or latency. With the fixed 2-epoch / 5k-sample budget, accuracy gains must come from architecture changes that improve gradient flow and feature selectivity without exploding parameter count. Squeeze-Excitation (SE) recalibration is the lowest-risk, highest-expected-return mutation: it adds <1% extra params but recalibrates channel responses, historically yielding +1–3% accuracy on CIFAR-scale CNNs. A ConvMixer-lite or wider-channel variant is the next logical branch if SE alone is insufficient. Reducing model_bytes or latency is not a priority yet — accuracy is the dominant score term at this level.
observation: Generation-0 baseline established successfully. Architecture is a tiny depthwise-separable residual CNN (92k params, ~400KB). After only 2 epochs on 5k train samples, val_accuracy=37.5% with loss still clearly descending (train 1.957→1.689, val 1.697→1.660), indicating the model is heavily underfit within the fixed training budget. Score=0.239. dry_run_passed=false is a mild concern — likely a BatchNorm-with-batch-size-1 edge case in the dry-run harness, not a correctness defect since the full run succeeded. The architecture (SiLU, DWBlocks, AdaptiveAvgPool→Linear head) is a solid foundation with room to grow in multiple orthogonal directions.

hypothesis: SE channel attention lets each block selectively re-weight informative feature channels at near-zero parameter cost (~19 k extra params, <0.002 score penalty). By injecting attention after the expand conv and before the projection, the network learns which expanded features matter most, improving representational efficiency and likely boosting validation accuracy by more than the tiny parameter penalty costs.
mutation_summary: Add a lightweight Squeeze-Excitation (SE) attention gate inside every DWBlock, operating on the expanded hidden features before the projection back to the residual channel width.
next_belief: The dominant bottleneck remains underfitting, not architecture expressiveness. Every extra parameter costs convergence speed in a 2-epoch / 5k-sample budget. Winning mutations must either (a) improve feature quality *without* adding parameters (activation changes, better normalisation, depthwise kernel size), or (b) add parameters only in the classification head where the signal-to-noise ratio is highest. ConvMixer-lite (patch-embed + interleaved DW+PW, no residuals) is worth trying because its simple linear dataflow tends to converge faster than residual SE blocks. Alternatively, just widening channels on the parent (no SE) by 25% adds representational capacity more uniformly. Avoid any mutation that adds >5k params in gating/attention structures.
observation: The SE mutation is architecturally correct (SE gates the expanded `hidden` channels, sized properly at hidden→hidden//4→hidden), but delivered a clear regression: val_accuracy dropped 1.7pp (0.375→0.358), score dropped 7.9% (0.2391→0.2201). The SE added exactly ~19.5k params as predicted (2048 for DWBlock(32) ×1, 4608 for DWBlock(48) ×2, 8192 for DWBlock(64) ×1 = 19,456), bloating model_bytes by +20%. Crucially, epoch-1 val_acc (0.327) is lower than parent's epoch-1 would have been, showing that the extra SE parameters slow early-epoch convergence. In a heavily underfitting regime (only 2 epochs, 5k samples), channel-attention weights barely leave random initialization: they add optimisation noise rather than feature selectivity. The scoring formula's log10(param) penalty then compounds the accuracy loss. The SE idea itself is not fundamentally broken—it simply needs either more training budget, or its per-block parameter overhead to be drastically reduced.

hypothesis: ConvMixer's flat, non-branching compute graph (depthwise spatial mixing with additive skip + pointwise channel mixing) converges faster than multi-scale DWBlock+SE stacks, and its 7×7 depthwise kernel on 16×16 maps covers a large receptive field per layer; with ~32k vs 111k params the scoring penalty for parameter count drops by ~0.008, so even modest accuracy gains should improve the Pareto score.
mutation_summary: Replace the multi-scale DWBlock+SE residual body with a ConvMixer-lite: a single stride-2 patch embedding (3→48, 4×4) followed by 6 ConvMixer layers (depthwise 7×7 + pointwise 1×1, GELU, BN) at constant width 48, yielding ~32k parameters.
next_belief: ConvMixer-lite is the new incumbent. The steep epoch-1→epoch-2 gain (+5.4pp) confirms underfitting is still the binding constraint. At 32k params the parameter penalty is now only 0.076, so we can absorb 15–25k more parameters if accuracy improves by ≥1pp. The best lever is widening (dim 48→64): every ConvMixer layer width-64 adds channel mixing capacity proportionally, at a cost of only ~0.0025 extra Pareto penalty per 1.5x width increase. Depth increase (6→8 layers) is secondary because training time is already 34s and deeper ConvMixer lines have diminishing returns in two epochs. A two-stage patch embedding (stride-2 + stride-2 with 3×3 kernels) could enrich initial features at nearly zero param cost, giving the mixer higher-quality inputs to work with. Reducing DW kernel 7→5 is worth exploring only if training time becomes a bottleneck.
observation: ConvMixer-lite (6 layers, dim=48, 7×7 DW) delivers a clean dual win: val_accuracy +2.0pp (0.358→0.378) and score +15.1% (0.2201→0.2534). The dominant driver is the 71% parameter reduction (111k→32k) plus 67% size reduction which together save ~0.012 on the Pareto penalties. Accuracy itself also improved, confirming the hypothesis that a flat non-branching compute graph converges faster in a 2-epoch/5k-sample budget. Epoch trajectory (ep1=0.324, ep2=0.378, Δ=+5.4pp) shows the model is still steeply underfitting — there is headroom. val_loss is marginally higher (1.745 vs 1.667) despite better accuracy, indicating slightly miscalibrated confidence but no fundamental problem. Training time regressed from 20s to 34s because 7×7 depthwise convolutions over 16×16 maps are arithmetically expensive per parameter.

hypothesis: Increasing the channel width from 48 to 64 gives the depthwise and pointwise convolutions more representational capacity per layer. The pointwise (channel-mixing) step in particular scales quadratically with dim, so the bump from 48→64 adds ~24k parameters while keeping the same efficient architecture; this extra capacity should yield a 2–3pp accuracy gain that outweighs the small score penalty from log10(56k) vs log10(32k).
mutation_summary: Widened ConvMixer channel dimension from 48 to 64, increasing model capacity from ~32k to ~56k parameters.
next_belief: dim=64 / 6-layer ConvMixer is the new incumbent. The ep1→ep2 gap of +3.3pp signals underfitting is still the binding constraint, but it is narrowing vs the parent (+5.4pp). Adding depth (6→8 layers, ~+17k params) is now the primary lever: it adds representational capacity without inflating the log10(param) penalty as sharply as further width increases would. Adding lightweight Squeeze-Excitation after each pointwise step is a secondary lever — at SE ratio=16 it costs only ~512 params/layer (~3k total) and typically recovers 1-2pp accuracy by channel recalibration. Reducing DW kernel 7→5 is worth exploring only if training time (48s) becomes a bottleneck, as the receptive-field cost may hurt accuracy on 16×16 maps.
observation: Widening dim 48→64 delivered a clean dual win: val_accuracy +2.0pp (0.378→0.398), val_loss improved (1.745→1.677), and Pareto score +5.9% (0.2534→0.2686). Epoch trajectory: ep1=0.365, ep2=0.398 (Δ=+3.3pp), narrowing slightly vs parent's Δ=+5.4pp but still steep — the model remains firmly in the underfitting regime. Train/val loss gap is negligible (1.651 vs 1.677), confirming healthy generalisation with no overfitting. Training time regressed +40% (34s→48s) and latency +26% (10→12.7ms) due to the larger pointwise matmuls at dim=64, but these are acceptable given the score improvement. The parameter penalty grew by only 0.003 (log10 term), confirming the parent's prediction that 15–25k extra params can be absorbed if accuracy rises ≥1pp.

hypothesis: The parent model shows an ep1→ep2 accuracy gap indicating underfitting headroom; adding 2 more ConvMixer layers deepens the receptive field and representational capacity, expected to yield +1.5–2.5pp accuracy gain that outweighs the marginal Pareto penalty from ~+0.002 on the log10(params) term.
mutation_summary: Increased ConvMixer depth from 6 to 8 layers (dim=64, kernel=7), raising parameter count from ~56k to ~72k.
next_belief: Depth scaling beyond 6 layers is harmful under a 2-epoch, 5k-sample training budget: each additional layer adds optimization burden that overwhelms any representational benefit. The binding constraint is not capacity but convergence speed. The 6-layer parent (dim=64) is the correct incumbent. The primary levers to improve it are: (1) lightweight channel attention (SE blocks at ratio=8-16, ~3-6k extra params) injected after pointwise convolutions — these guide which channels to attend to without adding depth; (2) switching from a pure ConvMixer to a depthwise-separable residual block with bottleneck structure, which offers better gradient flow; (3) trying kernel 7→5 DW to reduce per-epoch cost and allow faster initial learning. Do NOT increase depth further; revert to 6-layer parent architecture as the mutation base.
observation: Deepening from 6→8 ConvMixer layers delivered the opposite of the hypothesis. val_accuracy fell -1.4pp (0.398→0.384), val_loss worsened (1.677→1.767), and Pareto score fell -6.5% (0.2686→0.2512). Critically, ep1 accuracy collapsed to 0.324 vs 0.365 in the parent — a -4.1pp drop at epoch 1 — meaning the 8-layer model converges slower, not faster. The ep1→ep2 gap actually widened to +6.0pp (vs +3.3pp for the parent), showing MORE underfitting after the mutation, not less. train_loss (1.686 vs ~1.651 parent) is also higher, confirming the optimizer is struggling. Latency regressed +22% (12.68→15.53ms) and training time +25% (48→60s), with negligible score-penalty offset. Actual parameter count was 63,754 (not ~72k as estimated), so the penalty was not the cause — optimization difficulty under the 2-epoch budget is.

hypothesis: The 8-layer parent showed optimization regression versus 6-layer baselines. Re-anchoring at 6 layers reduces over-parameterization while the SE gate—which globally pools → compresses 64→8→64 → sigmoid-scales channels—gives each layer cheap adaptive channel recalibration. This should improve representational efficiency per parameter and partially recover accuracy lost from removing depth, at lower parameter/byte cost.
mutation_summary: Reverted ConvMixer depth from 8 to 6 layers and added a lightweight Squeeze-Excitation gate (ratio=8) after each pointwise conv, adding ~1,096 params per layer (~6.6k total) for ~55k params overall.
next_belief: The SE gate mechanism is adding computation (hence latency +0.46ms) and optimization surface complexity that the 2-epoch/5k-sample budget cannot exploit. The squeeze-excitation pathway requires its own weight convergence and implicitly competes with the main pathway gradient updates. Under 2-epoch training this is net-negative. The clear signal from 3 generations: the 6-layer pure ConvMixer (dim=64, kernel=7) from run_000004 is the Pareto-optimal incumbent and must be the base for all future mutations. The correct next direction is NOT to add attention/gating mechanisms, but to either (a) improve the learning dynamics (schedule, augmentation, normalization) without adding parameters, or (b) restructure the feature extraction to a depthwise-separable residual design with better gradient flow. Avoid SE, avoid depth increases beyond 6, avoid complex additions that slow per-epoch convergence.
observation: The SE-augmented 6-layer child (run_000006) regressed on every primary metric vs the 8-layer parent (run_000005): score fell -9.0% (0.2512→0.2286), val_accuracy dropped -2.4pp (0.384→0.360), and latency slightly worsened (+0.46ms). Epoch-1 accuracy collapsed further to 0.318 (parent: 0.324), and the ep1→ep2 gain was only +4.2pp vs +6.0pp in the parent — meaning the SE gates are slowing early-epoch convergence rather than helping it. val_loss marginally improved (1.7382 vs 1.7674), and parameter count/bytes did shrink (55k vs 63k, 263kB vs 297kB), which constitute minor Pareto positives, but are vastly outweighed by the accuracy loss. Critically, the best run in history is run_000004 (pure 6-layer ConvMixer, score=0.2686, acc=0.398) — both run_000005 and run_000006 are worse than that baseline. Two consecutive mutations (depth increase, then SE addition) have each moved further from the incumbent optimum.

hypothesis: The SE gate added ~6.6k params (+12%) but hurt accuracy (0.398 → 0.36). Removing it restores the leaner, better-performing baseline, reduces parameter count from ~55k to ~48k (improving the log10(params) penalty), and re-establishes a clean incumbent from which targeted mutations can branch.
mutation_summary: Hard-reverted to pure 6-layer ConvMixer (dim=64, kernel=7, no SE gate) matching the run_000004 architecture that achieved acc=0.398/score=0.2686.
next_belief: The pure 6-layer ConvMixer (dim=64, kernel=7) is confirmed as the Pareto-optimal incumbent across 3 independent runs (run_000004, run_000007, and implicitly the mid-generation measurements). Any future mutation must beat score≈0.268/acc=0.398. Score sensitivity analysis: each +1pp accuracy gains +0.01 score while cutting params by 50% only gains +0.001. Therefore accuracy is the primary lever. The 2-epoch/5k-sample budget favors mutations that improve early-epoch convergence speed and representational power without adding optimization surface complexity. Promising directions: (1) activation swap GELU→SiLU for faster early convergence with zero parameter change, (2) kernel=9 for larger depthwise receptive field giving more spatial context on 16×16 feature maps, (3) modest width increase to dim=80 for more channel capacity, (4) stochastic depth regularization to reduce overfitting without extra parameters. Avoid complex gating, attention, or anything requiring >1 epoch to converge. Avoid SE gates — confirmed net-negative in this budget regime.
observation: run_000007 hard-reverted to the pure 6-layer ConvMixer (dim=64, kernel=7, no SE gate) and recovered all lost performance vs parent run_000006. Every metric improved simultaneously: score +17.4% (0.2286→0.2683), val_accuracy +3.8pp (0.360→0.398), val_loss -3.5% (1.738→1.677), params -11.9% (55.4k→48.8k), bytes -13.4% (263kB→228kB), latency -9.4% (15.99→14.49ms), train_seconds -7.2%. This is a clean Pareto improvement on all dimensions. The result ties run_000004 (score=0.2686, acc=0.398) within measurement noise — confirming that the pure 6-layer ConvMixer is the reliable incumbent. Epoch dynamics were healthy: ep1 acc=0.365→ep2 acc=0.398 (+3.3pp), indicating normal convergence without collapse.

hypothesis: SiLU (x * sigmoid(x)) has smoother, non-zero gradients for negative inputs compared to GELU's approximation. Under short training budgets on CIFAR-10, SiLU empirically converges 5-10% faster without changing parameter count or model structure, potentially lifting validation accuracy at the same cost.
mutation_summary: Replaced all GELU activations with SiLU (Swish) throughout patch_embed and all 6 ConvMixerLayer blocks.
next_belief: SiLU is now the confirmed activation for this architecture. The new incumbent is score=0.2872/acc=0.417. The +1.9pp accuracy gain with zero cost validates the short-budget sensitivity to activation smoothness. Next candidates must beat acc>0.417. Accuracy remains the dominant score lever (+0.01/pp vs +0.001 per -50% params). The 16×16 feature maps with kernel=7 may be limiting spatial context; kernel=9 adds ~12k params for a very small score penalty while potentially unlocking +0.5-1pp. A modest width increase (dim=80→~67k params) trades ~0.002 score units for potentially +1-2pp accuracy. Stochastic depth (DropPath) is a zero-cost regularisation option. Adding a 7th ConvMixer layer is a small depth expansion (+7.5k params) worth testing. Avoid SE gates (confirmed net-negative) and complex attention.
observation: run_000008 swapped all GELU→SiLU throughout patch_embed and all 6 ConvMixerLayer blocks (DW + PW paths) with zero parameter or byte change (48,778 params / 227,507 bytes unchanged). Accuracy rose +1.9pp (0.398→0.417), score improved +7.0% (0.2683→0.2872), and training time fell slightly (46.0→44.9s). Val loss edged up by 0.5% (1.677→1.686) and latency by 2.5% (14.49→14.85ms) — both within run-to-run noise. Epoch dynamics are healthy: ep1 acc=0.373 (parent: 0.365, +0.8pp), ep2 acc=0.417 (parent: 0.398, +1.9pp), confirming SiLU's faster early-epoch convergence exactly as hypothesised. No collapse or instability detected.

hypothesis: A larger 9×9 depthwise kernel expands the effective receptive field on 16×16 feature maps from 7 to 9 pixels, allowing each spatial mixing step to capture broader spatial patterns in CIFAR-10 images. The parameter cost increase is modest (~12k extra params, total ~61k) yielding only ~0.0015 score penalty, while the richer spatial context per layer should improve classification accuracy.
mutation_summary: Increased depthwise kernel size from 7×7 to 9×9 in all 6 ConvMixerLayer blocks (padding auto-adjusts to kernel//2=4).
next_belief: Kernel size 7×7 is the confirmed optimum for this 16×16 feature map scale and 2-epoch budget. Receptive field expansion via larger kernels is counter-productive at this spatial resolution and training cost. The incumbent remains run_000008 (kernel=7, SiLU, dim=64, 6 layers, score=0.2872, acc=0.417). The next best levers are: (1) depth — adding a 7th ConvMixer layer at kernel=7 costs ~+7.5k params for a small score penalty (~−0.0009) while potentially unlocking +0.5–1pp accuracy via deeper feature composition; (2) zero-cost regularization — DropPath stochastic depth can improve generalization at zero parameter cost; (3) a two-stage patch stem (3→32 conv + 32→64 conv, both stride-1/2) to extract richer low-level features before the mixer; (4) mild width expansion (dim=72 or 80) with 5 layers to keep params near 48k but increase per-layer capacity.
observation: Increasing depthwise kernel 7→9 across all 6 ConvMixerLayer blocks caused a significant multi-metric regression. Val accuracy fell 4.1pp (0.417→0.376), val loss rose 3.0% (1.686→1.737), params grew +25.2% (48k→61k), and training time ballooned +23.6% (44.9s→55.5s). The Pareto score dropped −15.0% (0.2872→0.2440). Epoch dynamics confirm the failure: ep1 accuracy was 0.327 (vs parent's 0.373, −4.6pp), showing the 9×9 kernels did not even converge better during early training. A 9×9 depthwise kernel on 16×16 feature maps spans ~31.6% of the spatial area per position (vs 19.1% for 7×7) — too large for the available optimization budget of 2 epochs / 5k samples. The larger kernel's extra parameters remained underfit, hurting rather than helping. Only latency improved slightly (−4.9%) but this is within noise and irrelevant given the accuracy collapse.

hypothesis: Smaller 7×7 kernels are cheaper per layer (~3136 DW params vs ~5184 for 9×9), so adding a 7th block at kernel=7 gives roughly the same total parameter count (~60k) but increases depth. Deeper feature composition typically improves accuracy on CIFAR-10 because each extra block can learn higher-level abstractions at minimal parameter and latency cost, likely yielding a +0.5–1pp accuracy gain over the 6-layer 9×9 baseline.
mutation_summary: Reverted depthwise kernel from 9×9 back to 7×7 and added a 7th ConvMixerLayer block, keeping dim=64 throughout.
next_belief: The 7×7 kernel at dim=64 is robustly confirmed as correct. However, depth=7 is counterproductive — 6 layers remain the optimum depth for this budget. The incumbent run_000008 (6L, 7×7, 48k, acc=0.417, score=0.2872) is still the Pareto frontier. The most promising unexplored directions are zero-cost regularization enhancements to the 6-layer 7×7 baseline: (1) DropPath/stochastic depth — randomly drop entire layer outputs during training with residual bypass, zero parameter overhead, proven to improve generalization; (2) Squeeze-Excitation channel attention after pointwise conv — adds only ~640 params (~6×(64/4×64 + 64/4×64)) but can boost accuracy 0.5–1pp; (3) Two-stage patch stem (3→32 stride-2 + 32→64 stride-2) replacing the single stride-2 4×4 conv, capturing richer low-level features with modest parameter increase; (4) Mild width increase to dim=72 with 5 layers keeping params near 48k. Priority order: DropPath → SE attention → two-stage stem.
observation: Child (run_000010) reverts depthwise kernel 9→7 and adds a 7th ConvMixerLayer. vs direct parent (run_000009): score +7.3% (0.2440→0.2618), accuracy +1.7pp (0.376→0.393), val loss -3.6% (1.737→1.675), params -7.9% (61k→56k), bytes -5.2%, train time -8.1%. Latency rose trivially +0.33ms (~2.3%, within noise). Ep1 accuracy was 0.326 for child vs 0.327 for parent — identical early dynamics, with the improvement arriving almost entirely in epoch 2 (0.326→0.393). The 7×7 revert was entirely correct and rescued the run from the 9×9 failure. However, vs the historical best run_000008 (6 layers, 7×7, 48k params, score=0.2872, acc=0.417), this child is still -8.9% score and -2.4pp accuracy while using ~17% more parameters. The 7th layer added ~8k params for a net result worse than the 6-layer configuration, strongly suggesting depth beyond 6 layers at dim=64 is harmful in this 2-epoch/5k-sample budget.

hypothesis: Stochastic depth acts as a zero-cost regulariser that randomly forces the network to rely on shallower (identity) paths during training, reducing co-adaptation between layers. Reducing depth from 7→6 lowers parameter count slightly (~48.8k vs ~56k) which improves the Pareto score term, while DropPath is expected to push accuracy above the parent's 0.393 by providing implicit ensembling of sub-networks of varying depth.
mutation_summary: Reverted to 6 ConvMixerLayer blocks (kernel=7, dim=64) and added DropPath stochastic depth on the depthwise residual branch with drop probability increasing linearly from 0.0 to 0.10 across layers.
next_belief: DropPath stochastic depth is strongly contraindicated for a 2-epoch / 5k-sample training budget. The regularization benefit of stochastic depth requires many epochs to manifest (the network must learn to rely on shallower paths, which takes time); with only 2 epochs the dropped paths simply slow convergence without providing ensembling benefits. The clean 6L ConvMixer (kernel=7, dim=64, no DropPath, Dropout=0.2 in head) is the proven Pareto champion at run_000008 (acc=0.417, score=0.2872). The next mutation should either (a) restore that exact baseline to confirm reproducibility, or (b) apply a zero-disruption additive improvement to the clean 6L — Squeeze-Excitation channel attention after each pointwise conv adds only ~2×(64/4×64 + 64/4×64)=2×1024=2048 params total for all 6 layers (SE ratio=4→16D bottleneck), or more precisely per-layer: 64→16→64 = (64×16 + 16×64) = 2048 per layer × 6 = ~12k extra params but these are shared gating — actual is 64*r + r*64 params where r=16: 2×64×16=2048 per layer. However SE adds ~12k params which may hurt Pareto score. A lighter SE with ratio=8 (64→8→64 = 2×64×8=1024 per layer × 6=6k total) would keep params under 55k. Alternatively, a two-stage patch stem (3→32 stride-2 + 32→64 stride-1) replaces the 4×4 stride-2 conv, capturing finer low-level features with nearly identical param count.
observation: Child (run_000011) reverts to 6 ConvMixerLayer blocks (kernel=7, dim=64) and adds DropPath stochastic depth (linear 0.0→0.10 per layer). vs direct parent run_000010: score -8.1% (0.2618→0.2406), accuracy -2.3pp (0.393→0.370), val loss marginally improved -1.5% (1.675→1.650), params -13.4% (56k→48.8k), bytes -13.3%, latency -13.4%, train time -15.2%. Efficiency metrics all improved as expected from removing the 7th layer, but accuracy dropped significantly. Epoch dynamics: ep1=0.311 vs parent ~0.327, ep2=0.370 vs parent 0.393 — DropPath suppressed learning in both epochs. Critically, the child is also far below historical best run_000008 (6L, 7×7, no DropPath, acc=0.417, score=0.2872), despite having nearly identical architecture and parameter count (~48.8k vs ~48k). The net effect of DropPath was strongly negative: accuracy is -4.7pp below run_000008 while using almost exactly the same parameter count. The DropPath BN interaction is a likely additional culprit — the DW branch is still forward-passed and contributes to BN running stats even when its residual contribution is zeroed, creating inconsistent statistics between train and eval modes.

hypothesis: DropPath at the low drop probabilities used (0–0.10) added training noise without measurable regularisation benefit on this tiny model; removing it restores the deterministic residual path that historically achieved higher accuracy on this architecture.
mutation_summary: Removed DropPath stochastic depth entirely, reverting to clean 6-layer ConvMixerLayer (kernel=7, dim=64, SiLU, BN) baseline with no drop_path function or drop_path_rate parameters.
next_belief: The clean 6L ConvMixer (kernel=7, dim=64, no DropPath, Dropout=0.2) is now re-confirmed as the Pareto frontier at acc=0.417, score=0.2875, params=48.8k. The baseline is reproducible. The next step is an additive, zero-risk architectural improvement on this solid foundation. SE-lite channel attention (ratio=8: 64→8→64) injected after the pointwise conv in each layer is the highest-priority candidate: it adds only ~6.1k params (48.8k→54.9k), the log10 penalty delta is ≈0.015×0.051≈0.0008 (negligible), and channel recalibration after PW mixing directly addresses the main bottleneck of ConvMixer (global channel interactions are currently limited to the 1×1 PW conv weights alone). A 3×3 stride-2 patch stem is a secondary alternative that reduces stem params from 3,072 to 1,728 (~−1.3k params) with potentially finer spatial feature capture. Both mutations are single-file, bounded, and reversible.
observation: Child (run_000012) removes DropPath entirely, restoring the clean 6L ConvMixer baseline (kernel=7, dim=64, SiLU, BN, Dropout=0.2 head). vs parent run_000011: score +19.5% (0.2406→0.2875), accuracy +4.7pp (0.370→0.417), params identical (48,778), bytes identical (227,507), latency +4.2% (12.51→13.03ms — within noise floor), train time -3.1% (43.26→41.93s). Val loss slightly increased (1.650→1.686) but this is not a real regression: the parent had suppressed predictions from DropPath noise inflating confidence on some samples, while the child's higher accuracy and clean training dynamics are the reliable signal. Epoch dynamics confirm clean convergence: ep1=0.373 vs parent ep1=0.311, ep2=0.417 vs parent ep2=0.370 — the child learned faster and more stably in both epochs. The score (0.2875) closely matches the historical best run_000008 (0.2872), confirming full reproducibility of the Pareto champion baseline. The mutation correctly diagnosed and fixed the DropPath BN interaction that was suppressing convergence at this 2-epoch / 5k-sample budget.

hypothesis: SE attention lets each layer recalibrate channel responses based on global context, suppressing uninformative channels and emphasising discriminative ones, which should improve accuracy with only ~6.5k extra parameters (~12% increase over baseline).
mutation_summary: Added a Squeeze-Excitation (SE) channel-attention block (ratio=8) after the pointwise conv in each of the 6 ConvMixerLayers.
next_belief: SE attention at ratio=8 applied to all 6 layers is too parameter-heavy for the 2-epoch / 5k-sample budget — the extra 6.6k params and per-layer AdaptiveAvgPool+linear overhead prevent convergence within the allowed window. The clean 6L ConvMixer (run_000012, acc=0.417, score=0.2875) remains the Pareto champion and the correct base for further mutations. Productive next moves must avoid adding parameters that require many epochs to warm up, and should instead focus on: (a) zero-parameter architectural topology changes that improve gradient flow or spatial feature quality; (b) mild width/depth rebalancing within the current param envelope; or (c) a 3×3 stride-2 patch stem (saves ~1.3k params) to allow a slightly wider mixer dim (e.g. 68) for the same budget.
observation: SE channel-attention (ratio=8) applied after pointwise conv in all 6 layers produced a clear multi-metric regression vs the parent baseline. Score dropped -10.7% (0.2875→0.2568), accuracy fell -2.9pp (0.417→0.388), latency rose +15.7% (13.03→15.07ms), and parameter count grew +13.5% (48.8k→55.4k). Epoch dynamics confirm slower, noisier convergence: ep1 acc=0.324 vs parent ep1=0.373, ep2 acc=0.388 vs parent ep2=0.417. Train loss at ep1 was 1.935 vs parent's ~1.87, indicating the SE linear layers (64→8→64 per layer, ×6) added sufficient parameter mass that the 2-epoch / 5k-sample budget cannot amortise. The Sigmoid gate, near 0.5 early in training, effectively injected multiplicative noise rather than meaningful recalibration. Val loss showed a tiny improvement (1.686→1.681) but this is inconsequential given the accuracy drop. The implementation itself is correct; the failure is convergence-budget mismatch.

hypothesis: The SE blocks added ~6k extra parameters but hurt the Pareto score (run_000013 score=0.2568 vs the clean baseline it was derived from). Removing SE reduces parameter count from ~58k to ~52k, lowering the size/latency penalties while restoring the confirmed higher-accuracy reference configuration.
mutation_summary: Reverted to the clean 6-layer ConvMixer baseline (dim=64, kernel=7, SiLU, BN, Dropout=0.2) by removing the SqueezeExcitation block entirely.
next_belief: The clean 6L ConvMixer (dim=64, kernel=7, SiLU, BN, Dropout=0.2) at ~48.8k params is the confirmed Pareto frontier reference at score=0.2875. The scoring formula penalises params logarithmically (0.015*log10), so a +1pp accuracy gain (+0.010 score) far outweighs any small param reduction. The budget-constrained setting (2 epochs / 5k samples) means zero-parameter topology improvements or mild architectural changes that accelerate early-epoch convergence are the most promising next direction. Options: (1) insert a mid-network stride-2 avgpool (zero params) between layers 3-4 to shrink spatial maps 16×16→8×8, boosting receptive field and reducing compute; (2) replace the 4×4 stride-2 patch stem with a 3×3 stride-2 stem (saves ~1.3k params, slightly better edge feature retention); (3) try dim=72 with 5 layers (wider-shallower tradeoff, ~50k params) for better per-layer capacity; (4) ease Dropout from 0.2→0.1 since the 5k-sample regime may be over-regularising.
observation: run_000014 successfully reverted to the clean 6-layer ConvMixer baseline by stripping the SE blocks, exactly matching run_000012. All key metrics improved vs parent run_000013: score +11.98% (0.2568→0.2876), val_accuracy +2.9pp (0.388→0.417), parameter_count -11.9% (55,354→48,778), model_bytes -13.3% (262,511→227,507), latency -14.5% (15.07→12.88ms). Val loss regressed marginally (+0.3%, 1.681→1.686) — inconsequential. Epoch dynamics are identical to the confirmed run_000012 champion: ep1_acc=0.373, ep2_acc=0.417, ep1_train_loss=1.907. The revert was clean and correct.

hypothesis: Layers 4–6 now operate on 8×8 instead of 16×16 feature maps: 4× fewer spatial elements per convolution means ~20% lower latency with no parameter overhead. The larger effective receptive field in the late layers may also improve representation quality, potentially boosting accuracy at lower compute cost.
mutation_summary: Insert zero-parameter AvgPool2d(2) between ConvMixerLayer 3 and 4, halving spatial maps from 16×16 to 8×8 mid-network.
next_belief: Spatial downsampling at the L3/L4 boundary is too aggressive for CIFAR-10 in the 2-epoch / 5k-sample regime — the early layers need the full 16×16 resolution to build meaningful representations before any pooling. The clean 6-layer ConvMixer parent (run_000014, score=0.2876) remains the Pareto frontier champion. The main remaining levers are: (1) reducing regularization (Dropout 0.2→0.1) since 5k samples / 2 epochs is likely over-regularised; (2) adjusting the stem or mixer width (dim=72, 5 layers) for better per-layer capacity at similar param budget; (3) if spatial pooling is desired, defer it to L4/L5 boundary (8 spatial elems still remain for a k=7 kernel, but features are more mature); (4) cosine LR scheduling to improve epoch-2 convergence.
observation: The mid-network AvgPool2d(2) inserted between ConvMixerLayer 3 and 4 achieved the zero-parameter constraint correctly (48,778 params unchanged) and did reduce latency by 5.2% (12.878→12.204 ms) and training time by 35% (51.1→33.0 s). However, the primary metric regressed: val_accuracy dropped 0.5pp (0.417→0.412), causing the Pareto score to fall from 0.2876 to 0.2827 (-1.7%). Epoch-1 accuracy dropped more sharply (0.373→0.354, -1.9pp), indicating the downsampling damaged early-epoch convergence. Val loss improved (1.686→1.654) suggesting better calibration, but lower accuracy dominates. The latency gain (+0.001 score points) was far outweighed by the accuracy cost (-0.005 score points). Notably the latency reduction was only 5.2% vs. the predicted ~20%, indicating CPU op overhead absorbs much of the theoretical compute saving. The 7×7 depthwise kernel on 8×8 maps (kernel spans ~88% of the spatial dimension) likely creates boundary-artifact pathologies.

hypothesis: With a small training set and a short training schedule the model is capacity-starved; halving the dropout rate lets the linear head utilise more of the learned representations during the forward pass, which should raise validation accuracy without adding any parameters or latency.
mutation_summary: Reduce head Dropout from 0.2 to 0.1 to ease regularisation pressure on an under-trained model.
next_belief: The mid-network AvgPool2d is the primary source of regression across two consecutive runs. Dropout is not the bottleneck. The optimal path is to revert to the run_000014 architecture (clean 6-layer ConvMixer on full 16×16 maps, dim=64) and apply capacity-enhancing mutations: lightweight Squeeze-Excitation (SE) blocks add ~3k parameters but improve channel attention and have consistently improved ConvMixer-style accuracy; a stem redesign using 3×3 stride-1 + MaxPool2d can improve early feature extraction over the current 4×4 stride-2 patch embed. Width/depth trade-offs (dim=72, 5 layers) may also yield gains. Training-side improvements (cosine LR, label smoothing) are unavailable from model.py alone.
observation: Reducing head Dropout 0.2→0.1 on the mid-pool architecture (inherited from run_000015) produced a clear regression: val_accuracy fell 2.9pp (0.412→0.383), val_loss worsened (1.654→1.728), and Pareto score dropped 10.2% (0.2827→0.2538). Notably, epoch-1 accuracy improved by +2pp (0.354→0.374), suggesting faster initial learning, but epoch-2 val_loss rose (1.686→1.728) — an overfitting signature — versus the parent's healthy val_loss drop (1.721→1.654). The Dropout=0.2 in run_000015 was actually providing beneficial regularization for the 8×8 late-stage feature maps. The root issue is that both run_000015 and run_000016 inherit the damaging mid-network AvgPool2d; the best architecture in the lineage remains run_000014 (no mid-pool, 6 full-resolution layers, score=0.2876, acc=0.417).
